Group A — Yogurt Study: Table 1 and Group Comparisons

Author

Workshop Participant

In this session we’ll pick a biological question, build a Table 1 to describe the participants (yogurt vs. unchanged diet), then compare groups using gtsummary and ggpubr with p-values.

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(gtsummary)
library(ggpubr)

# Load all four datasets
sample_ids  <- read_csv("../../data/group_a_yogurt/00_sample_ids_yogurt.csv")
participants <- read_csv("../../data/group_a_yogurt/01_participant_metadata_yogurt.csv")
qpcr        <- read_csv("../../data/group_a_yogurt/02_qpcr_results_yogurt.csv")
luminex     <- read_csv("../../data/group_a_yogurt/03_luminex_results_yogurt.csv")

# Join sample info to qPCR and Luminex
qpcr_joined <- sample_ids |>
  left_join(qpcr, by = "sample_id")

luminex_joined <- sample_ids |>
  left_join(luminex, by = "sample_id")

Step 1 — What biological question interests your group?

Write your group’s question here:

Your question: ___________________________________________________________________

Step 2 — Build a Table 1

A Table 1 summarises the demographic and baseline characteristics of participants, split by study group. This lets us check if the groups are similar at the start of the study.

Code 
# tbl_summary with defaults
participants |>
  select(-pid, -sex) |>  # remove ID and constant columns
  tbl_summary(by = arm)
Characteristic unchanged_diet
N = 251		 yogurt
N = 261
days_since_last_sex 6 (3, 10) 5 (3, 11)
birth_control

    Depoprovera 8 (32%) 17 (65%)
    no hormonal birth control 17 (68%) 9 (35%)
age 30.0 (28.0, 33.0) 31.0 (29.0, 33.0)
education

    grade 10-12, matriculated 6 (24%) 4 (15%)
    grade 10-12, not matriculated 9 (36%) 12 (46%)
    less than grade 9 4 (16%) 5 (19%)
    post-secondary 6 (24%) 5 (19%)
1 Median (Q1, Q3); n (%)
Code 
# Add p-values to see if groups already differ at baseline
participants |>
  select(-pid, -sex) |>
  tbl_summary(by = arm) |>
  add_p()
Characteristic unchanged_diet
N = 251		 yogurt
N = 261		 p-value2
days_since_last_sex 6 (3, 10) 5 (3, 11) 0.8
birth_control

0.017
    Depoprovera 8 (32%) 17 (65%)
    no hormonal birth control 17 (68%) 9 (35%)
age 30.0 (28.0, 33.0) 31.0 (29.0, 33.0) 0.6
education

0.8
    grade 10-12, matriculated 6 (24%) 4 (15%)
    grade 10-12, not matriculated 9 (36%) 12 (46%)
    less than grade 9 4 (16%) 5 (19%)
    post-secondary 6 (24%) 5 (19%)
1 Median (Q1, Q3); n (%)
2 Wilcoxon rank sum test; Pearson’s Chi-squared test; Fisher’s exact test
Code 
# Polished version with overall column and bold labels
participants |>
  select(-pid, -sex) |>
  tbl_summary(by = arm) |>
  add_p() |>
  add_overall() |>
  bold_labels() |>
  modify_header(label = "**Characteristic**")
Characteristic Overall
N = 511		 unchanged_diet
N = 251		 yogurt
N = 261		 p-value2
days_since_last_sex 5 (3, 11) 6 (3, 10) 5 (3, 11) 0.8
birth_control


0.017
    Depoprovera 25 (49%) 8 (32%) 17 (65%)
    no hormonal birth control 26 (51%) 17 (68%) 9 (35%)
age 31.0 (28.0, 33.0) 30.0 (28.0, 33.0) 31.0 (29.0, 33.0) 0.6
education


0.8
    grade 10-12, matriculated 10 (20%) 6 (24%) 4 (15%)
    grade 10-12, not matriculated 21 (41%) 9 (36%) 12 (46%)
    less than grade 9 9 (18%) 4 (16%) 5 (19%)
    post-secondary 11 (22%) 6 (24%) 5 (19%)
1 Median (Q1, Q3); n (%)
2 Wilcoxon rank sum test; Pearson’s Chi-squared test; Fisher’s exact test
Code 
# Override: show age as mean (SD) with a t-test
participants |>
  select(-pid, -sex) |>
  tbl_summary(
    by = arm,
    statistic = list(age ~ "{mean} ({sd})")
  ) |>
  add_p(test = list(age ~ "t.test")) |>
  add_overall() |>
  bold_labels() |>
  modify_header(label = "**Characteristic**")
Characteristic Overall
N = 511		 unchanged_diet
N = 251		 yogurt
N = 261		 p-value2
days_since_last_sex 5 (3, 11) 6 (3, 10) 5 (3, 11) 0.8
birth_control


0.017
    Depoprovera 25 (49%) 8 (32%) 17 (65%)
    no hormonal birth control 26 (51%) 17 (68%) 9 (35%)
age 30.7 (3.3) 30.4 (3.3) 31.0 (3.3) 0.5
education


0.8
    grade 10-12, matriculated 10 (20%) 6 (24%) 4 (15%)
    grade 10-12, not matriculated 21 (41%) 9 (36%) 12 (46%)
    less than grade 9 9 (18%) 4 (16%) 5 (19%)
    post-secondary 11 (22%) 6 (24%) 5 (19%)
1 Median (Q1, Q3); n (%); Mean (SD)
2 Wilcoxon rank sum test; Pearson’s Chi-squared test; Welch Two Sample t-test; Fisher’s exact test

Interpretation: Are the two groups similar at baseline? Any variables where p < 0.05? What does that mean for our study?

Step 3 — Compare outcomes between groups

Now let’s look at the actual study outcomes. We’ll compare the yogurt group vs. the unchanged diet group using different bacterial and cytokine measures.

3a — Total bacterial load (qPCR)

Code 
# Boxplot of total bacterial load at each time point
qpcr_joined |>
  ggplot(aes(arm, qpcr_bacteria, fill = arm)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  facet_wrap(~ time_point) +
  labs(
    title = "Total bacterial load by group",
    subtitle = "Faceted by time point (baseline vs. after antibiotic)",
    y = "Total bacterial DNA copies",
    x = ""
  ) +
  stat_compare_means(method = "wilcox.test") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

3b — L. crispatus (protective bacterium)

Code 
qpcr_joined |>
  ggplot(aes(arm, qpcr_crispatus, fill = arm)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  facet_wrap(~ time_point) +
  scale_y_log10() +  # log scale because data spans many orders of magnitude
  labs(
    title = "Lactobacillus crispatus by group",
    subtitle = "Y-axis on log scale",
    y = "L. crispatus DNA copies (log scale)",
    x = ""
  ) +
  stat_compare_means(method = "wilcox.test") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

3c — L. iners (less stable species)

Code 
qpcr_joined |>
  ggplot(aes(arm, qpcr_iners, fill = arm)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  facet_wrap(~ time_point) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(
    title = "Lactobacillus iners by group",
    y = "L. iners DNA copies (log scale)",
    x = ""
  ) +
  stat_compare_means(method = "wilcox.test") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

3d — Cytokine levels (pick a few)

Code 
# Panel of key cytokines, only within-limits measurements
cytokines_of_interest <- c("IL-1a", "IL-1b", "IL-6", "IL-8", "IL-10", "TNFa")

luminex_joined |>
  filter(cytokine %in% cytokines_of_interest, limits == "within limits") |>
  ggplot(aes(arm, conc, fill = arm)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  facet_wrap(~ cytokine, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = "Cytokine concentrations by group",
    subtitle = "Only within-limits measurements shown",
    y = "Concentration (pg/mL)",
    x = ""
  ) +
  stat_compare_means(method = "wilcox.test") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Step 4 — Paired analysis: baseline vs. after antibiotic

Each participant was sampled at two time points, so we can also look at how things change within each group.

Code 
# Prepare the data for paired analysis
qpcr_paired <- qpcr_joined |>
  filter(time_point %in% c("baseline", "after_antibiotic")) |>
  mutate(time_point = fct_relevel(time_point, "baseline", "after_antibiotic"))

# Line plot: total bacteria, pre vs. post, by group
qpcr_paired |>
  ggplot(aes(time_point, qpcr_bacteria)) +
  geom_line(aes(group = pid), alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_point(aes(color = arm), size = 2) +
  facet_wrap(~ arm) +
  labs(
    title = "Change in total bacterial load: baseline → after antibiotic",
    y = "Total bacterial DNA copies",
    x = ""
  ) +
  stat_compare_means(
    method = "wilcox.test",
    paired = TRUE,
    comparisons = list(c("baseline", "after_antibiotic"))
  )

Step 5 (Optional) — Adjusted analysis

We can also run a simple linear model that adjusts for baseline differences.

Code 
# Only run this if you want to adjust for age or other variables
outcome <- qpcr_joined |>
  filter(time_point == "after_antibiotic") |>
  left_join(participants, by = c("pid", "arm"))

lm(log10(qpcr_bacteria) ~ arm + age, data = outcome) |>
  tbl_regression()

What does your group conclude?
